channel.title: Pornography for Posterity
channel.description: Recent content on Pornography for Posterity

item.title: Contribute
item.description: PFP is maintained by volunteers and the record it’s trying to keep is much larger than what’s catalogued so far. Useful contributions include: Corrections — a wrong date, a misattributed alias, a studio credit that doesn’t match the source material. Sources — trade press, box art, official studio releases, or other public documentation that would support a new or existing entry. New entries — performers or studios not yet catalogued, especially from the project’s initial pre-digital-era focus (see History & Scope Notes).

item.title: Frequently Asked Questions
item.description: Isn’t this all already documented somewhere? Partially. General-purpose industry databases exist and PFP relies on them as sources. What’s missing is a project treating this specifically as historical preservation rather than as a current, commercially-oriented directory — one concerned with what gets lost as studios close, sites go offline, and performers from earlier eras are forgotten, rather than only what’s current. Why does this matter? It’s just pornography. “Just” is doing a lot of work in that sentence.

item.title: History & Scope Notes
item.description: Why the pre-digital era first Commercial pornographic film has a documentable production history stretching back decades before the internet — a relatively small number of studios, shot on film and later analog video, distributed through theaters, mail order, and video rental. That era has a natural boundary: it ended, and the record of it is fixed in size even as pieces of it are actively being lost. Studios closed and their archives scattered or were destroyed.

item.title: Methodology
item.description: How entries are built Each performer or studio entry starts from publicly available industry records — trade databases, contemporaneous press coverage, official studio credits, and, where available, the performer’s own public statements about their own career. Entries record: Names and aliases a performer or studio worked under, since stage names changed frequently and the same person is often catalogued under several unconnected names elsewhere. Studio and release information — what was produced, by whom, and when.

item.title: About the Pornography for Posterity Project
item.description: What this project is Pornography for Posterity (PFP) is an independent, volunteer-run effort to catalog and preserve the historical and biographical record of commercial pornographic film and photography — performers, studios, releases, and the surrounding context that made them significant. It exists because pornography is one of the most widely consumed and least documented media categories in modern history. Newspapers do not run obituaries for its performers. Libraries do not accession its releases.
